To meet halfway

Although we were nine days late, that does not explain a three-month slip.
Our records show March, whereas the baseline called for January.
Despite the late approval, we kept the line moving.
We accept the drawing delay. However, the schedule tells a different story.

João, thank you for the call, but I have to be direct. Your notice of three missed milestones ignores the real cause: Motiva approved our drawings nine days late, and our line could not start without them.

Thank you, Petra, and let me start by conceding your point. Although we approved the revised drawings nine days late, and I accept that openly, I'd like us to look at the whole timeline together rather than trade blame.
I appreciate that you admit it. So we agree the delay was on your side.

On the January milestone, yes. Whereas the February and March milestones slipped after your approval was already in hand, so those two sit with GleisTech. Despite the nine-day delay, the schedule shows the later slips began on your line.
But your team approved the compressed schedule in January, you accepted the risk yourselves.

I'm not saying your team dropped the ball, Petra, I'm saying the schedule shows a gap, and I'd like us to close it together. Even though we accepted a tight schedule, the two later slips are documented against your delivery dates, not ours.
Fair. Then the sticking point is who pays for the recovery shifts. We cannot absorb that alone.

Then let's table the cost for now and agree the delivery sequence first, so nobody has to lose face to move forward. I'll meet you halfway on the January shifts; however, the February and March recovery sits with your team. A joint remediation plan that holds each side accountable, and we protect two years of goodwill.
Although we were late on one revision, that does not explain the whole delay.
I'm not saying you dropped the ball; I'm saying the schedule shows a gap.
Let's find the common ground first, then draw the line where the evidence draws it.
I can commit to a remediation plan. However, I can't accept sole responsibility.
Despite the tension, I'd rather meet you halfway than lose a month to a dispute.

João, before the call with the supplier this afternoon, let me give you the picture, because the numbers matter more than the noise. GleisTech has missed three delivery milestones in a row, and this morning they sent a letter claiming the whole delay was caused by our late approval of the technical drawings. Now, part of that is true, and I want you to concede it openly. We were nine days late on one revision in January. But nine days does not explain a three-month slip, and the baseline schedule proves it. So here is what I need from you. Do not go in swinging, and do not roll over either. Acknowledge the drawing delay, because it is real and pretending otherwise costs us all our credibility. Then hold the line on the rest, with the schedule in front of you. We are not trying to win an argument this afternoon. We are trying to leave with a remediation plan and a supplier who still wants to work with us on the next project. Push back on the claim, not on the people. Find the common ground first, then draw the line where the evidence draws it.

Right, three things before you sit down for a conflict call, because engineers are usually either too soft or too hard, and both lose. First, separate the person from the problem. The moment you say you failed, the other side stops listening and starts defending, and you will never reach a remediation plan through a closed door. Say the schedule shows a gap, not you dropped the ball. It is the same fact, but one of them keeps the room open. Second, concede early and concede something real. If you were nine days late on a drawing, say so in the first two minutes. Giving ground on a small, true point is not weakness; it buys you enormous credibility when you hold firm on the big one. The engineer who admits nothing is never believed on anything. Third, always leave the other side a way to save face. Nobody signs a remediation plan that reads like a confession. Offer a path where they can commit to the fix without being publicly humiliated, and you will get the signature. Assertive is not aggressive. Assertive means you say the hard thing clearly, and you say it in a way the other person can still walk back from. Draw the line, but build the bridge across it at the same time.
